text: The surgeon encounters difficulty in maintaining exposure due to the patient's large size and high BMI, opting for a lateral to medial approach to access the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The small bowel is visible and challenging to keep out of the surgical field.
The surgeon marks the presumed safe proximal margin on the sigmoid colon, ensuring a reference point for resection after mobilization.
The surgeon suspends the uterus to improve exposure in the surgical field during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the lateral mobilization of the sigmoid colon, working from lateral to medial due to difficulty visualizing the root of the sigmoid mesentery.
The surgeon uses suction in the left hand while approaching the area near the ureter, which becomes visible in ICG mode.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection in an attempt to open a natural tissue plane, encountering adhesions to the left adnexa. A fistula is observed connecting to the left adnexa.
The surgeon attempts to pull the sigmoid colon distally from the left anterior pelvis, addressing a small abscess visible in the left adnexa.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to create space and further expose the sigmoid colon, which is nearly out of the pelvis.
The surgeon successfully lifts the tissue off the sidewall, completing the dissection and freeing the sigmoid colon from its adhesions.

The surgeon places a sponge in the surgical field due to oozing and proceeds with lateral mobilization of the colon. The redundant descending colon is visible, allowing for sufficient length without mobilizing the splenic flexure.

The patient is positioned in approximately 26 degrees of Trendelenburg with the left side elevated. The surgeon inspects the area to identify the majority of the diverticulum to be removed, deciding to stop at a specific point. The small bowel and pelvis are visible, indicating some difficulty in exposure.
The surgeon creates a small window beneath the bowel wall, focusing on areas with diverticulum while avoiding removal in the proximal, wider diameter colon.
The surgeon navigates beneath the bowel wall and the thickened mesentery, which is folded down to the rectosigmoid junction, preparing for potential transrectal extraction.
The small bowel is visibly encroaching into the pelvic area, presenting a challenge for maintaining a clear surgical field.

The surgeon compares the compliance of the orifice to the rectum, expressing confidence in the integrity of the specimen and otomy edges. The surgeon proceeds to staple proximally, preferring this method for joining the two staple lines to create an anastomosis.
The surgeon struggles to move the small bowel aside to gain a clear view of the deep pelvis for the extraction procedure, while preferring the technique involving staple lines.

The surgeon inserts a hand to maneuver the specimen into the pelvis, with the small bowel visible deep in the pelvic region. An EEA sizer is placed high towards the cervix to facilitate the posterior opening in the back of the vagina. Using a ring clamp, the specimen is extracted easily compared to transrectal extraction, with the vaginal opening clearly visible deep in the pelvis.
The surgeon uses a 2-0 V-Loc suture to close the defect created during the natural orifice extraction, running the suture back and forth to secure the closure.

The surgeon repositions the small bowel over the sacral promontory using an arm to prevent it from obstructing the surgical field. The surgeon then runs a stitch back before proceeding to create the anastomosis.

The surgeon creates an opening in the rectum and proximal bowel, joining the two staple lines with two PDS sutures. The tissue lips light up green, indicating good perfusion. The surgeon carefully avoids making the opening too far laterally to prevent involving the mesentery. The inner layer is sewn using a three OV lock suture, running towards the patient's left.
The surgeon brings the stitch to the patient's right side and ties the two sutures together, completing the anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the running of two PDS sutures as the second outer layer across the top of the anastomosis site. Preparations are made to conduct a leak test on the bowel anastomosis.
